title: The Hundred-Year Egg
content:
The alley off Kuanzhai Alley stank of wet cardboard and cat piss at two in the morning. Street vendors had packed up their skewers an hour ago. The only light came from a single red paper lantern swinging above a door that had no business being there — sandwiched between a shuttered noodle shop and a wall covered in peeling Mao-era propaganda posters.

The sign read: 夜厨. Night Kitchen. No English translation. No phone number. No hours posted.

Lin Hao wiped down the single prep counter with a rag that had seen better decades. His knife roll lay unrolled on the cutting board — eight blades, each forged by a swordsmith in Longquan who thought he was making kitchen tools for a madman. The steel had a faint blue sheen that caught the fluorescent light.

Six tables. Twelve chairs. A stove that ran hotter than any gas line in Chengdu had a right to. The exhaust fan wheezed overhead, sucking the steam from a pot of bone broth that had been simmering for seventy-two hours straight.

The bell above the door jingled.

Lin Hao didn't look up. He knew the sound of mortal footsteps — heavier, sloppier, always dragging a little behind the body like they couldn't quite catch up.

But this one was different.

The man who walked in wore a rumpled suit jacket over a stained undershirt. His eyes had the hollowed-out look of someone who'd been staring at spreadsheets for eighteen hours straight. Or crying. Or both. His tie was loosened to the point of surrender.

"You open?" The man's voice cracked.

Lin Hao gestured at the empty tables. "Does it look open?"

The man blinked at the dim lighting. The single candle on each table. The kitchen where the only sound was bubbling broth and the occasional hiss of steam.

"I mean... are you serving?"

Lin Hao picked up his cleaver. Tested the edge against his thumbnail. It parted the nail like butter. "I'm always serving. Question is whether you're ready to eat."

The man sat at the nearest table without being invited. His hands shook as he reached for the menu — a single sheet of rice paper with characters so faint they seemed to float above the surface.

He stared at it for a long time.

"I can't read this."

"Then you shouldn't be here."

The man's jaw tightened. "I heard about this place from a friend. He said you make things that... fix people." He laughed, bitter and broken. "I don't know what I expected. Some kind of miracle cure. That's stupid."

Lin Hao set down his cleaver. Wiped his hands on his apron. "What's wrong with you?"

"Everything."

"That's not specific enough for a menu item."

The man pressed his palms flat against the table. His knuckles were white. "I lost my wife two years ago. Cancer. She was thirty-four. I've been sleeping in my office because I can't stand being in our apartment. I quit my job last week. I haven't eaten anything that wasn't delivered in three months. My landlord is evicting me tomorrow. I have three hundred yuan to my name."

He looked up. His eyes were wet but he wasn't crying. Like he'd run out of tears somewhere around month fourteen.

"I don't want a miracle. I just want to feel something that isn't this."

Lin Hao studied him. The exhaustion in his shoulders. The way his breath came shallow and quick, like he'd forgotten how to breathe properly. The faint tremor in his hands that could be caffeine withdrawal or grief or both.

The chef turned and walked to the cold storage unit in the back. Not the main fridge — the small one behind the hanging pork carcasses, the one with the lock that required a key he kept on a chain around his neck.

Inside was a clay crock, sealed with wax and tied with red string. The label on top was written in a hand that predated the Republic.

百年蛋. Hundred-year egg.

But that was a bad translation. In truth, it meant something closer to: *prepared across a century.*

He lifted the crock carefully. The clay was warm to the touch, even though the storage unit ran at near freezing. Inside, submerged in a mixture of clay, ash, salt, and lime, were a dozen eggs. Each one had been buried in 1923. Each one had absorbed something from the earth it rested in. Each one had developed a soul of its own.

Lin Hao cracked one into a bowl.

The yolk was dark green, almost black. The white had turned amber and translucent, like old honey. The smell was rich and sulfuric — a hundred years of patience concentrated into a single bite.

He thought about killing it with technique. Serving it as a traditional cold appetizer with pickled ginger. That's what any chef would do.

Instead, he grabbed the leftover mapo tofu from the night before. The block had been sitting in chili oil and Sichuan peppercorns, absorbing heat and numbness until every cube was a tiny bomb of sensation.

He diced the century egg fine. Folded it into the tofu. Topped it with fresh scallions and a sprinkle of toasted sesame.

The man stared at the bowl when Lin Hao set it down.

"This is... mapo tofu?"

"With a twist."

"I didn't order mapo tofu."

"You ordered something with qi. This has the most I'm willing to give a mortal."

The man picked up his chopsticks. His hand was still shaking. The first bite he took was small, tentative — the cautious approach of someone who'd forgotten what real food tasted like.

Then his eyes went wide.

The mapo tofu hit first — the numbing Sichuan peppercorns that made his lips tingle and his tongue dance. The chili oil followed, spreading heat through his chest like a fire being stoked. But underneath it all was something else. Something ancient and patient and deep.

The century egg.

It didn't taste like egg. It tasted like the earth after rain. Like the inside of a temple at dawn. Like the moment between inhale and exhale when you realize you're still alive.

"Chef," the man whispered. "This egg. How old is it?"

Lin Hao lit a cigarette. He didn't smoke often, but some moments demanded it. "Old enough to remember when this city was still a walled town. Old enough to have been laid by a bird that never saw an airplane. Old enough that the man who buried it died in the Sino-Japanese War."

The man's chopsticks stopped halfway to his mouth.

"Are you serious?"

"Does it matter?"

He ate the rest of the bowl in silence. But something changed as he ate. The tremor in his hands subsided. His breathing slowed. The hollow look in his eyes began to fill with something — not joy, not yet, but the possibility of it. A door cracking open.

When the bowl was empty, he set down his chopsticks and exhaled. "I feel... strange."

"You'll feel stranger in about five minutes. The qi needs time to settle."

"What happens after five minutes?"

Lin Hao took a long drag of his cigarette. "You'll remember what it felt like to be whole. The effect lasts about three days. Long enough to get your shit together. Maybe find a therapist. Definitely stop sleeping in your office."

The man stared at his hands. They were still now. Completely still.

"Why did you do this for me? You don't know me."

"I don't need to know you." Lin Hao crushed out his cigarette on the counter. "I can smell grief on people. It has a specific odor, like iron and rainwater and something rotting. You walked in reeking of it."

"And you just... fix that?"

"I gave you a pause button. What you do with the time is your business."

The man reached for his wallet. Lin Hao held up a hand.

"Don't. You have three hundred yuan to your name. You need that more than I do."

"I have to pay something."

"Pay it forward. Or don't. I don't care." Lin Hao turned back to his prep counter. "The door's that way."

The man stood up. Walked to the door. Turned back.

"Will you still be here? If I come back?"

Lin Hao picked up his cleaver. Tested the edge again. The blade caught the candlelight and threw it across the room like a blade of fire.

"I'm always here. Question is whether you'll be ready to eat what I'm serving next time."

The door swung shut. The bell jingled.

Lin Hao stood alone in his kitchen, surrounded by six empty tables and twelve empty chairs and a stove that burned hotter than any mortal fire had a right to.

He looked at the mapo tofu pot. The century egg shells in the trash. The small clay crock in the back, now down to eleven eggs.

Eleven more chances.

Eleven more customers.

Then he'd have to bury another batch. And the ingredients for that weren't easy to come by.

The phone on the wall rang — a vintage rotary model that shouldn't have worked, let alone received calls at three in the morning.

Lin Hao let it ring three times. Then picked it up.

"You're late," he said.

The voice on the other end was silk and smoke and something ancient. "The kitchen in Heaven has been complaining about you again. They say you're poaching their supply."

"I don't poach. I forage."

"Same difference, Hao. The Jade Emperor is starting to take notice. You might want to be careful about who you serve."

Lin Hao looked at the empty bowl on table three. The residual qi still shimmering in the porcelain like heat rising off asphalt.

"I'll be careful when they stop dying."

He hung up.

The red paper lantern flickered outside. Somewhere in the alley, a cat screamed. The bone broth bubbled on the stove.

And in the cold storage unit behind the hanging pork carcasses, eleven century eggs waited in their clay cocoons, dreaming of the moment they would finally be cracked open and released.

title: Chapter 2
content:
CHAPTER 2: The Tooth Collector

The kitchen clock read 2:47 AM when Lin Hao heard the bell above his door chime.

He didn't look up from the mandolin. The blade sang through a thousand-year-old ginseng root, each translucent slice curling onto the cutting board like amber glass. The fragrance alone could cure a common cold. Or raise the dead, depending on the root's age.

"Menu's on the wall," he said. "No substitutions. No refunds. Payment upfront."

The footsteps that crossed his dining room made no sound. Not the whisper of rubber soles on tile. Not the click of heels. Just the pressure of weight shifting through air, as if the floor refused to acknowledge the presence above it.

Lin Hao set down his knife.

The woman who sat at his counter looked human enough. Late twenties. Shoulder-length black hair. A face that would be pretty if not for the hunger in her eyes. She wore a business suit, navy blue, expensive cut. Her hands were folded on the marble countertop, fingers interlaced, knuckles white.

"I need the Fire-Feather menu," she said.

Lin Hao wiped his hands on his apron. "You know what that costs?"

"I know what it does." Her voice cracked on the last word. "My mother has three days. The hospital says her marrow is already dead. The hematologist suggested I start making arrangements."

"Then you should be making arrangements."

The woman's composure shattered. Her hands slammed against the counter. The marble didn't crack, but Lin Hao felt the vibration through his soles. "I have money. I have property. I have—"

"You have a dying mother." Lin Hao reached for the kettle. "I'm sorry. Truly. But the Fire-Feather menu requires a Vermilion Bird feather. I don't have one in stock. And even if I did, I charge in memories, not currency."

"I'll give you anything."

"Anything?" He poured green tea into a porcelain cup. The leaves spiraled in the hot water, dancing their ancient dance. "Have you ever eaten a Vermilion Bird's feather, Ms...?"

"Zhao. Zhao Liling."

"Have you, Ms. Zhao?"

She shook her head.

"The first bite burns," Lin Hao said. "Not your mouth. Your soul. You'll feel every sin you've committed, every lie you've told, every kindness you failed to offer. The second bite shows you every moment you could have been better. The third bite..." He slid the tea toward her. "The third bite either rebuilds your mother's marrow or turns her to ash. The feather doesn't discriminate between disease and curse. It burns away everything impure. Including the patient, if the impurity runs too deep."

Zhao Liling's hands trembled around the teacup. "She's a good woman. She raised me alone after father left. She worked three jobs. She never—"

"Every child believes their mother is good." Lin Hao's voice was gentle, which made it worse. "The feather doesn't care about belief."

The bell chimed again.

Lin Hao's shoulders tightened. Two customers in one night. That hadn't happened since the Mid-Autumn Festival, when the Jade Rabbit stopped by for a late-night snack and brought three hungry ghosts as plus-ones.

The second customer was a man. Old. Bent-backed. He wore a gray changshan that had been expensive eighty years ago and threadbare ever since. His eyes were milk-white, filmed over with cataracts or something older. He carried a wooden cane carved with characters so ancient they predated the Shang dynasty.

He sat at the far end of the counter, three seats away from Zhao Liling.

"Master Lin," the old man said. His voice sounded like stones grinding together at the bottom of a river. "I've waited sixty-three years for a reservation."

Lin Hao studied him. The old man's aura was wrong. Not demon. Not spirit. Something in between, something that had been human once and then kept going past the point where humanity should have stopped.

"I don't take walk-ins," Lin Hao said.

"The waiting list."

"Is three centuries long."

"I know." The old man smiled. His teeth were too white, too straight, too young for his wrinkled face. "I'm patient, Master Lin. I've had time to learn patience. But my window is closing. Three days. That's all I have left."

Three days. Same as Zhao Liling's mother.

"Coincidence is a dish I don't serve," Lin Hao muttered.

"Pardon?"

"Nothing." He turned back to Zhao Liling. "Ms. Zhao, I'm afraid I must ask you to leave. I have a new customer to attend to, and—"

"I'm not leaving."

"This isn't a negotiation."

"Then give me something else." She grabbed his wrist. Her grip was iron. "Anything. A soup. A tea. Something that can buy time. I'll pay whatever you ask. I'll give you my memories of her. Every birthday party. Every bedtime story. Every time she kissed my forehead and said she loved me. Take them all. I don't care if I forget her face. I just want her to live."

Lin Hao looked down at her hand. Pale fingers wrapped around his forearm. A mortal's grip, but fueled by desperation that could move mountains.

"The Kitchen God's Mushroom Soup," he said slowly. "It won't cure her. But it might slow the decay. Buy you a week. Maybe two."

"How much?"

"Your first memory of your father."

Zhao Liling went still.

"Before he left," Lin Hao continued. "The one good memory. The one you hold onto when you tell yourself he wasn't all bad. I want that."

"You're a monster."

"I'm a chef. The ingredients don't grow on trees." He pulled his wrist free. "Do we have a deal?"

She was silent for a long moment. Then she nodded.

Lin Hao reached into his apron pocket and pulled out a small leather pouch. Inside was a dried mushroom the size of his thumbnail, black as ink, with veins of gold running through its cap. He'd found it growing on the corpse of a minor dragon in the mountains outside Chengdu. It had taken him three days to convince the dragon's spirit to let him harvest it.

He dropped the mushroom into a mortar and began to grind.

The pestle moved in circles, crushing the mushroom into powder. Golden sparks rose from the mortar with each rotation. The smell that filled the kitchen was impossible to describe—earth and sky and death and rebirth, all mixed together.

"Your father," Lin Hao said. "Think of him."

Zhao Liling closed her eyes. Her face cycled through emotions too fast to track. Anger. Sadness. Longing. And then, finally, a softness that made her look ten years younger.

"I was four," she whispered. "He came home from work early. Took me to the park. Pushed me on the swings until my legs got tired. Then he bought me ice cream. Strawberry. My favorite. He sat next to me on the bench and let me tell him about my day. Everything. Every detail. He listened like I was the most important person in the world."

Lin Hao felt the memory flow into the mortar. It tasted like strawberry ice cream and summer evenings and the safety of a father's attention.

He poured the mushroom powder into a clay pot, added water from a spring that only existed during solar eclipses, and set it over the flame. The soup would take ten minutes. In that time, he would have to deal with the old man.

"You said three days," Lin Hao said, turning to face him. "What happens in three days?"

The old man tapped his cane against the floor. Tap. Tap. Tap. Each impact sent ripples through the air, disturbing the dust motes that floated in the kitchen's dim light.

"Three days from now, the Celestial Bureaucracy will hold a review of my case. My existence has been... irregular. I was supposed to die in 1959. A heart attack. Quick. Painless. But I had unfinished business. So I made a deal with a hungry ghost. Extended my life. Extended it too far, it seems."

"You made a deal with a hungry ghost?" Lin Hao's hand stopped mid-stir. "Those creatures are bottomless. They'll eat your soul piece by piece until there's nothing left."

"I know." The old man's milky eyes seemed to focus on something far away. "I've paid pieces every year. My sense of smell. My taste buds. My ability to feel joy. My left kidney. My right lung. Every year, another payment. But now the hungry ghost wants the final piece. My soul. And when the Celestial Bureaucracy reviews my case, they'll see what I've done. They'll erase me from existence. No reincarnation. No afterlife. Just... nothing."

Lin Hao stirred the soup. The golden sparks had settled into a soft glow, pulsing like a heartbeat.

"What do you want from me?"

"Feed me something that will make me strong enough to fight."

"I don't serve weapons."

"Then feed me something that will make me fast enough to run."

"I don't serve escapes."

The old man's composure cracked. His hands, gnarled and spotted with age, gripped his cane until the wood groaned. "Then what do you serve?"

"Food." Lin Hao ladled the mushroom soup into a bowl. "I serve food. It heals or it harms depending on who eats it. It grants power or takes it away depending on what I put into it. But I don't choose the outcome. The dish chooses."

"You're a coward."

"I'm a cook." He slid the bowl toward Zhao Liling. "Drink. Slowly. Let it settle before you leave."

She lifted the bowl with both hands. The steam curled around her face, and for a moment, Lin Hao saw her mother reflected in her eyes—a woman with the same stubborn jaw, the same desperate love.

Zhao Liling drank.

The effect was immediate. Color flooded back into her cheeks. Her shoulders straightened. The exhaustion that had carved lines into her face smoothed away.

"Your mother will have more time," Lin Hao said. "Use it wisely."

She set down the bowl. Tears streamed down her face, but she wasn't crying. The soup was forcing out impurities, flushing her system clean. "Thank you."

"Don't thank me. You paid the price."

She left without another word. The door chimed behind her.

Lin Hao turned to the old man. "You should leave too."

"I can't."

"Then you'll die here."

"Better here than in a celestial courtroom." The old man reached into his sleeve and pulled out a jade pendant. It was carved in the shape of a dragon, coiled around itself, teeth bared. "I have one more payment to offer. Not to the hungry ghost. To you."

Lin Hao's breath caught.

The pendant was authentic. He could feel its power from across the counter—ancient, hungry, screaming with the trapped soul of a minor dragon.

"That's worth more than anything on my menu."

"I know." The old man placed it on the counter. "I want the Dragon's Wrath Noodles."

Lin Hao's blood went cold.

"There are only two bowls of Dragon's Wrath Noodles in existence," he said. "One was eaten by a general during the Three Kingdoms period. He conquered nine cities before the meal ended. The other is sitting in my freezer, waiting for a customer who's willing to pay the price."

"I'm willing."

"The price isn't jade."

"Then what is it?"

Lin Hao leaned forward. His voice dropped to a whisper. "I need you to tell me how you survived sixty-three years past your death date

title: The First Bite of Heaven
content:
The kitchen clock read 1:47 AM when the qilin arrived.

Lin Hao heard it before he saw it — hooves striking cobblestone outside his alley, each step ringing like a temple bell. He set down his cleaver and wiped his hands on his apron. The meat thermometers on his station read 131 degrees for the dragon ribeye. Perfect medium-rare. It would have to hold.

The door chimed.

Most demons entered his restaurant with arrogance. They kicked the door open, or slithered through shadows, or materialized from clouds of sulfur and spite. The qilin simply walked in like a weary traveler who'd been walking for a thousand years and needed a seat.

It looked human enough. Tall, broad-shouldered, wearing a grey changshan that smelled of sandalwood and old scrolls. But its eyes burned amber, and when it breathed, tiny flames licked the air from its nostrils.

"I heard you serve the forbidden," the qilin said. Its voice crackled like embers.

Lin Hao gestured to a table by the window. "I serve what people order."

"I am not people."

"No. You're the last of your kind south of the Yangtze."

The qilin's eyes narrowed. Those flames in its nostrils grew brighter. "You know what I am and you do not run."

"Should I?"

"I could burn this building to ash with a thought."

Lin Hao picked up his cleaver again. The blade caught the low light — twenty inches of Damascus steel, folded sixty-four times by a swordsmith in Longquan who didn't know what the knife would be used for. Or maybe he did. The old man never asked questions.

"Then I'd be out of a job," Lin Hao said. "And you wouldn't get dinner. Sit. It's late. You look tired."

The qilin stared at him for a long moment. Then it sat.

"What's the special tonight?" it asked.

Lin Hao opened his cold storage. Steam rolled out, carrying the scent of aged meat and iron. He pulled out a tray covered in oiled paper. When he peeled the corner back, the flesh beneath gleamed like black pearl, marbled with veins of silver fat.

"Dragon ribeye," he said. "Yellow River dragon. Been dry-aging for six weeks. It's ready tonight."

"I've eaten dragon before."

"Not like this."

The qilin leaned forward. Its amber eyes traced the marbling, the dark crimson of aged meat, the precise trimming around the edges. A chef's cut. Nothing wasted.

"How much?"

"For you? Free."

"I don't accept charity."

"It's not charity. I've been saving this piece for someone who'd appreciate it. Most of my customers just want the power. They don't taste the food."

"And you think I will?"

"You're a qilin. You've been alive for two thousand years. You've eaten everything this world has to offer. You tell me if I'm wrong."

Lin Hao didn't wait for an answer. He laid the steak on his cutting board, felt the familiar weight of the cleaver in his hand, and began.

The first cut was pure muscle memory — two hundred thousand repetitions in kitchens from Paris to Shanghai. He portioned the steak into eight-ounce medallions, each exactly two inches thick. The fat cap he trimmed and set aside for rendering. The bone he wrapped and saved for stock.

The qilin watched in silence. Somewhere in the distance, a temple bell struck twice.

Lin Hao heated his cast iron pan until the oil shimmered. He seasoned the steak with nothing but salt — Sichuan sea salt, harvested from ancient brine pools in the mountains. When the meat hit the pan, the sound was like a thunderclap. Steam erupted. The smell filled the kitchen — not just beef, but something older. River water. Morning mist. The anger of a dying god.

He seared each side for exactly ninety seconds. The crust formed dark and crackling. He added butter, rosemary, smashed garlic cloves. Basted. Flipped. Basted again.

"Is it true?" the qilin asked. "That you cook with divine technique?"

Lin Hao didn't look up. "I cook with French technique. The divinity is in the ingredients."

"You steal power from the dead."

"I use what would otherwise be wasted. The Celestial Bureaucracy kills rogue dragons every week. They burn the bodies. Thousands of years of accumulated essence, turned to ash. That's the real crime."

"You could be struck down for this."

"I could have been struck down a hundred times already." Lin Hao slid the steak onto a heated plate. He added a dollop of bone marrow butter, a sprinkle of micro herbs, a drizzle of aged balsamic that had cost more than most people's rent. "The First Kitchen doesn't care about me. I'm too small. And the Demon Court? They're my best customers."

He set the plate in front of the qilin.

It looked down at the steak. The butter was melting, running down the crust in golden rivulets. Steam carried the scent upward — beef fat, garlic, rosemary, and beneath it all, the unmistakable smell of dragon. Not the stink of scales and swamp, but the clean scent of storm clouds and lightning.

"One bite," the qilin said, "and I could level a city block."

"Yes."

"One bite, and I could burn through the gates of the Jade Palace."

"Probably."

"One bite, and I could — "

"You could enjoy your dinner," Lin Hao interrupted. "It's getting cold."

The qilin picked up its knife and fork. European style. Interesting.

It cut a single piece. The knife slid through the meat like it was cutting butter. The interior was perfect — dark pink, warm, glistening. The dragon's blood had been aged into something almost sweet.

It put the fork in its mouth.

For a moment, nothing happened.

Then the qilin's eyes went wide. Its pupils dilated. The amber glow in its irises flickered, dimmed, flared again. Its hands gripped the table edge so hard the wood groaned.

"Master," it whispered.

Lin Hao said nothing.

"I see — I see the river. The Yellow River. Before the dams. Before the pollution. When it ran clean from the mountains to the sea." The qilin's voice cracked. "I see him. The dragon. He was young. Only three hundred years. He was swimming upstream to spawn. He never made it."

"You're tasting his memory."

"I'm tasting his life."

The qilin took another bite. This time, it didn't speak. It just chewed, slowly, deliberately, with the reverence of a monk at prayer. Tears streamed down its face, evaporating into steam before they hit its collar.

Lin Hao busied himself with cleaning his station. He'd seen this before. Every customer had the same reaction — the first bite always unlocked something. A memory. An emotion. A power they didn't know they had.

The qilin finished the entire steak. It used the last piece of bread to wipe the plate clean. Then it set down its utensils and looked at Lin Hao with those burning eyes.

"You're not just a chef," it said.

"I'm just a chef."

"No. You're something else." The qilin stood. It was taller than Lin Hao remembered. "I came here to kill you."

"Most people come for the food."

"The Demon Court sent me. They said you've been cooking celestial creatures without proper tribute. They want you stopped."

"And now?"

The qilin reached into its changshan. Lin Hao's hand moved toward the knife block. But what the qilin pulled out wasn't a weapon. It was a piece of parchment, yellowed with age, sealed with red wax that bore the mark of three intertwined dragons.

"Now I'm giving you this."

Lin Hao took the parchment. He broke the seal. The script inside was classical Chinese, written in gold ink that seemed to glow. He read it once. Then again.

"What is this?" he asked.

"An invitation," the qilin said. "To the Celestial Kitchen."

"The what?"

"There's a competition. Every three thousand years. The gods of every pantheon send their best chefs to compete. The prize is a seat at the Table of Heaven — the right to cook for the highest divine beings in existence."

Lin Hao looked at the parchment. The gold ink was warm to the touch. "Why me?"

"Because in three thousand years of competition, no mortal has ever been invited." The qilin smiled, and there was something terrible in that smile. "And because the Demon Court wants you gone. They'd rather see you die in heaven than succeed on earth."

The qilin walked toward the door. At the threshold, it paused.

"One more thing, chef."

"What?"

"Your next customer is waiting outside. She's been there for three hours. She's a fox spirit. Nine tails. Very old. Very hungry."

"She could have come in."

"She doesn't eat meat."

Lin Hao frowned. "Then what does she eat?"

The qilin laughed — a sound like burning paper.

"Hope."

It stepped through the door and vanished into the night. The alley was empty. The street was empty. Even the shadows seemed to have retreated.

Lin Hao stood alone in his kitchen, holding an invitation to cook for gods, while a nine-tailed fox waited outside to eat something he didn't know how to make.

The clock struck two.

He opened the door.

A woman stood in the alley. She wore red — a qipao that seemed to shimmer with every movement. Her hair was black silk. Her eyes were ancient.

"Chef Lin," she said. Her voice was honey and knives. "I've been waiting."

"Come in."

She stepped past him. As she moved, the air stirred, and he caught the scent of jasmine and wet earth and something else — something that made his heart beat faster.

Behind her, nine tails flickered in the darkness, each one tipped with white fire.

"Tell me," she said, settling into the qilin's chair, "have you ever cooked a memory?"

Lin Hao looked at the invitation in his hand. Then at the fox spirit in his restaurant.

"No," he said.

She smiled.

"Tonight, you will learn."

title: The Last of the Thunderbirds
content:
The kitchen clock read 2:47 AM when the bell above the door jingled.

Lin Hao looked up from his prep station. The cleaver in his hand stilled, blade hovering over a slab of frozen Qilin marrow. Through the steam-streaked window of the pass, he watched a woman settle onto the center stool at his counter.

She wasn't a demon.

Demons had tells. The way they moved through the world demanded space, as if the air itself parted for them. The woman slipped onto the stool like she'd been there a thousand times before. No perfume. No jewelry. Just a grey Hanfu that caught the dim light like water over river stones.

She was old. Not in her face—her face could have been thirty, could have been fifty. The age lived in her eyes. Deep. Patient. The kind of patience that came from watching dynasties rise and fall while the soup simmered.

Lin Hao wiped his hands on his apron. "We're closed."

"Your sign says midnight to dawn."

"The sign also says reservations only. Wait list is three centuries."

"Three centuries, two months, and fourteen days." She smiled, and something in that smile made Lin Hao's fingers twitch toward the knife block. "I've been patient, Chef Lin. I don't intend to wait any longer."

He didn't ask how she knew his name. The supernatural crowd always knew his name. What bothered him was that he couldn't read her. Every demon, every spirit, every minor god that walked through his door left a residue—a faint heat signature in the spiritual spectrum, like a ghost of chili oil on the tongue.

This woman gave off nothing.

"What are you?" he asked.

"I'm hungry."

"Not what I meant."

"No." She folded her hands on the counter. "It's not."

Lin Hao set down his cleaver. He walked around the prep station and leaned against the pass, arms crossed. "Let me make this clear. I decide who eats here. I decide what they eat. And I decide what they pay. You don't get to walk in off the street and—"

"I have something you want."

He waited.

"A Leizi feather."

The air in the kitchen changed. The ambient heat from the wok burners seemed to press closer, denser. Lin Hao's jaw tightened.

Leizi. The Thunder Goddess. One of the few divine entities whose essence he'd never managed to acquire. Her feathers carried the weight of storms, the concentrated power of every bolt of lightning that had ever split the sky between heaven and earth. A single dish made from that ingredient would—

He stopped himself. "Leizi is still alive. You can't harvest from a living god."

"I didn't say I harvested it." The woman reached into her sleeve. Her hand emerged holding a small jade box, no larger than a matchbox. She placed it on the counter and slid it toward him. "I said I have one."

Lin Hao didn't touch the box. He'd learned that lesson the hard way, three owners of the restaurant ago, when a demon fox had offered him a "gift" that turned out to be a cursed bone designed to bind his soul to her service for a century.

"What do you want in return?"

"A meal."

"Everyone wants a meal. What meal?"

The woman's eyes flickered. For just a moment, the patience cracked, and Lin Hao saw something raw underneath. Something that looked almost like grief.

"A bowl of Songhua Dan," she said.

Lin Hao went cold.

Songhua Dan. Pine Flower Egg. A dish so old that most culinary texts only mentioned it in footnotes. The recipe called for a single preserved egg, aged for exactly one hundred years in a mixture of clay, ash, salt, and the powdered bones of a Fenghuang—a Chinese phoenix. The egg had to be buried at the base of a pine tree that had been struck by lightning exactly nine times.

And it had to be consumed on the night of a lunar eclipse, or the consumer would dissolve into a cloud of azure pollen and drift away on the next wind.

"I can't make that," Lin Hao said.

"Can't, or won't?"

"Can't. I don't have a hundred-year-old egg. I don't have Fenghuang bones. And even if I did, the preparation takes three days of continuous attention. I can't just—"

"I can get you the egg. And the bones."

Lin Hao studied her. The grey Hanfu. The steady hands. The grief behind her eyes. A woman who could acquire Leizi feathers and Fenghuang bones wasn't a woman who needed to barter for a meal. She could walk into any celestial kitchen in any of the Ten Courts and demand whatever she wanted.

"Why me?"

"Because you're the only one who would make it properly." She touched the jade box. "The others would add their own touches. Truffle oil. Foie gras. Gold leaf. They would try to improve it. You understand that some dishes are perfect exactly as they are."

Lin Hao understood. That understanding was why he'd left Paris. His mentor had screamed at him for three hours when he'd refused to deconstruct a classic Consommé Madrilène. "You have no vision!" the old man had shouted. "No creativity!"

Lin Hao had vision. He also had respect. There were dishes that had been perfected over centuries, refined by generations of hands that understood heat and time and patience in ways that Michelin stars could never measure. Those dishes didn't need improvement.

They needed preservation.

"Who are you?" he asked again.

The woman stood. She was taller than he'd realized. Not intimidating—nothing so crude. She simply occupied her space with the same inevitability that mountains occupied theirs.

"I'll return in three nights," she said. "With the ingredients. You'll have the feather as deposit."

She walked toward the door. Lin Hao picked up the jade box. It was warm in his palm, humming with a frequency that vibrated up his arm and settled behind his teeth like the taste of ozone.

"Wait."

She paused.

"If I make this dish," he said, "who am I serving it to?"

The woman turned. In the dim light of the Demon Kitchen, her face was half shadow, half jade, and fully unreadable.

"A ghost," she said. "My daughter."

The door closed. The bell jingled. And Lin Hao stood alone in his kitchen, holding a Leizi feather, wondering what the hell he'd just agreed to.

---

Three nights came fast.

Lin Hao didn't sleep. He prepped his station, sharpened his knives, and tried not to think about what he was about to do. Songhua Dan was forbidden. Not just because of the rare ingredients, but because of what the dish represented. An offering to the dead. A bridge between worlds. The celestial authorities had outlawed it during the Ming dynasty, after a high-ranking judge in the Court of the Dead had eaten one and spent the next three centuries wandering the mortal realm as a hungry ghost, unable to return to his post.

The woman arrived exactly at midnight.

She carried a leather satchel, stained dark with age. She set it on the counter and began unpacking. First, a clay jar sealed with red wax. Then a bundle wrapped in silk. Finally, a small wooden box that she placed at the very back of the counter, as if she didn't want to look at it.

"The egg," she said, tapping the jar. "Aged one hundred and seven years. Buried at the roots of a lightning-struck pine on Mount Tai."

Lin Hao cracked the seal. The smell that emerged was sharp and earthy, like mushrooms growing in a cave where no light had reached for centuries. The egg inside was black as ink, preserved in a crust of dark clay and pine ash.

"The bones," she said, unwrapping the silk.

Three ribs. White as snow, light as hollow bird bones, but dense with a power that made Lin Hao's teeth ache. Fenghuang bones. The real thing. He'd seen fakes before—pig bones painted with pearlescent dust—but these were genuine. The residual warmth of divine fire still clung to them.

"The last ingredient," she said.

She opened the wooden box.

Inside lay a single pine cone. Brown. Wilted. Unremarkable.

Lin Hao looked at it. Looked at her. "A pine cone."

"It has to be aged in the same clay as the egg. For exactly as long. This one was buried beside the egg for one hundred and seven years."

He touched it. The pine cone was cold. Not room-temperature cold, not refrigeration cold. Cold like the bottom of a well in January. Cold like something that had never known sunlight.

"You want me to serve this to your daughter," he said.

"Yes."

"She's a ghost."

"Yes."

"Songhua Dan is designed to anchor a spirit to the mortal realm. Permanently. She won't be able to cross over anymore. She'll be stuck here, in the living world, until the egg decays. That could be centuries."

The woman's face didn't change. "I know."

"And you're okay with that?"

"I'm her mother." The woman's voice cracked, just slightly. The first crack in her perfect composure. "I would rather have her here, trapped, than nowhere at all."

Lin Hao looked at the ingredients spread across his counter. The feather. The egg. The bones. The pine cone. He thought about the waiting list three centuries long, about the demons and spirits and gods who would kill for a single bite of what he was about to cook. He thought about the celestial authorities who would execute him if they found out.

Then he picked up his cleaver.

"Three days," he said. "Come back at midnight, three days from now."

The woman nodded. She turned to leave, then stopped at the door.

"Chef Lin."

"Yes?"

"When you serve her the dish," the woman said, "don't tell her what's in it. She doesn't know she's dead."

The door closed.

Lin Hao stood alone with a hundred-year-old egg, the bones of a phoenix, and the feather of a thunder goddess. Outside, the first rumble of distant thunder rolled across the Chengdu sky.

He began to cook.

---

Three days passed in a blur of heat and salt and concentration.

Lin Hao worked without stopping. He crushed the Fenghuang bones into a fine powder, mixed it with the clay from the egg jar, and rebuilt the preserving crust around the black egg. He placed it in a steamer basket lined with pine needles and set the water to a precise temperature—exactly ninety-eight degrees Celsius, never boiling, never cooling. He sat beside the steamer for eighteen hours, adjusting the heat with tiny movements of his wrist.

On the second day, he cracked the egg.

The yolk inside was green. Not mold-green. Jade-green. Translucent. It glowed faintly in the dim light of the kitchen, pulsing with a rhythm that matched Lin Hao's own heartbeat.

He separated the white from the yolk, each component requiring different treatment. The white he sliced into ribbons as thin as silk thread, each slice made at a specific angle that aligned with the magnetic currents of the earth. The yolk he wrapped in a single layer of cheesecloth and suspended over a bowl of rice wine vinegar, allowing it to absorb the vapor for twenty-four hours.

On the third day, he assembled the dish.

The pine cone he sliced into paper-thin rounds, each one revealing a spiral pattern that looked like a fingerprint made of wood. He arranged them in a circle on a celadon plate. On top of that, he layered the egg white ribbons, each one curled into the shape of a pine needle. In the center, he placed the yolk, now firm and amber, glowing like a miniature

title: The Butcher's Bill
content:
The kitchen phone rang at 2:47 AM.

Lin Hao ignored it. His hands were buried in the chest cavity of a nine-tailed fox, carefully separating the spirit core from the rib cage. The creature had been delivered fresh that morning—still warm, still leaking qi like steam from a pressure valve. He'd paid three hundred years of accumulated favor for this specimen.

The phone kept ringing.

"The line," Mei whispered from her station by the wok. "It's the red one."

Lin Hao's knife paused mid-cut. The red phone was mounted on the wall beneath a framed photo of his grandmother. It hadn't rung in seven years. He didn't know the number was still active.

He wiped his hands on his apron. Lifted the receiver.

"Chef Lin." The voice was silk wrapped around broken glass. Familiar. Wrong. "Congratulations on the fox. That's a rare find."

"I don't take congratulations from ghosts."

The Celestial Butcher laughed. It sounded like bones grinding. "I'm not dead yet, little chef. Though I hear you've been practicing on my suppliers."

Lin Hao's grip tightened on the phone. The Butcher had run the underground trade in mythological creatures for three centuries before the Heavenly Court caught him. They'd executed him publicly. Burned his soul to ash. Or so the stories said.

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"Don't insult me. You bought that fox from Wei the Nether Merchant. I owned Wei. I own everyone who moves product through the mortal realm." A pause. "Except you, apparently. You've been carving your own path. I respect that."

Lin Hao watched the fox's blood pool on his cutting board. It glowed faintly gold in the low light. "You're dead."

"I'm persistent." Another laugh. "And I have a proposal. One you'll want to hear before the Heavenly Enforcement Bureau kicks down your door tomorrow night."

The line went dead.

Lin Hao stared at the receiver for a long moment. The kitchen hummed around him—steam hissing from Mei's wok, the low thrum of the walk-in freezer, the drip of a faucet he'd been meaning to fix for three months.

"What did he want?" Mei asked. Her voice was carefully neutral. Chinese women of a certain generation had perfected that tone. It meant she was terrified.

"He wants to talk."

"He wants to use you." She turned back to her wok, but not before he caught the tremor in her hands. "The Butcher doesn't make proposals. He makes traps."

Lin Hao returned to his cutting board. The fox's spirit core sat exposed now, pulsing with pale light the color of autumn moon. Nine tails meant nine lifetimes of accumulated wisdom. The meat would be rich with memory.

He'd planned to serve it as a tasting menu. Nine courses, each one unlocking a fragment of the fox's knowledge. The diner would walk away with the secrets of a thousand years.

Now he wasn't sure he'd live long enough to plate the first course.

---

The Heavenly Enforcement Bureau arrived at exactly 10 PM the next evening.

Lin Hao had been expecting 9. He'd adjusted his schedule accordingly—the fox was broken down and curing in salt, the prep work finished, the dining room set with fresh flowers and clean linens. His grandmother's photograph had been turned to face the wall. No sense letting her watch this.

Three figures stepped through the front door. They didn't open it. They simply passed through the wood like smoke through a sieve.

The leader was a woman in a grey suit. Her hair was pulled back so tight it stretched her eyes into almonds. She carried nothing but a tablet, which she held up as she approached the counter.

"Lin Hao. Chef and proprietor of The Demon Kitchen." Her voice had no inflection. "You are charged with the unlicensed preparation and consumption of restricted spiritual creatures. Specifically: three lesser earth dragons, one qilin juvenile, two fenghuang eggs, and a nine-tailed fox of the lunar lineage."

"That's an impressive list." Lin Hao wiped down his counter. "Did you get it from the Butcher?"

The woman's eyes flickered. Barely a fraction of a second. But he caught it.

"The Butcher is deceased."

"Sure he is." Lin Hao set down his rag. "Then why does he have your phone number?"

The two enforcers behind her shifted. They were dressed like paramilitary—black armor inscribed with suppression talismans, weapons at their hips that glowed with celestial fire. They'd come ready to burn his restaurant to the ground.

The woman in grey didn't flinch. "You have two options, Chef Lin. You can surrender your kitchen and your records, submit to judgment by the Heavenly Court, and face a sentence of seven hundred years in the Jade Prison."

"And option two?"

"Option two is already in motion." She gestured at the tablet. "We confiscate everything. You resist. We erase you from existence. Your grandmother's soul will be notified."

Lin Hao felt something cold settle in his chest. It wasn't fear. It was the same feeling he got when a sauce broke and he had to start over. Disappointment, undercut by grim determination.

"Can I ask you a question?"

The woman tilted her head. Permission.

"If the Butcher is dead, who's been running the supply chain for the last seven years?"

Silence. The kitchen's ambient hum seemed to grow louder. The enforcers exchanged glances.

"The trade was dismantled," the woman said. But her voice had lost some of its edge.

"Was it?" Lin Hao reached under the counter. Both enforcers drew their weapons. He held up his hands, then slowly pulled out a leather-bound ledger. "I've been keeping records. Seven years of transactions. Suppliers. Buyers. Middlemen." He set the book on the counter. "Do you know how many of those names are dead?"

The woman stared at the ledger like it might bite her.

"Every single one," Lin Hao said. "Every supplier I've worked with has died within three months of our first transaction. Accident. Illness. Disappearance. The Butcher's been cleaning house. Making sure no one can trace the trade back to him." He tapped the book. "Except me. Because I'm the last one standing."

"Why would he let you live?"

"Because I'm not in the supply chain." Lin Hao smiled. It wasn't a pleasant expression. "I'm the customer. And you don't kill your customers. You kill the competition."

The woman's tablet beeped. She glanced at it, and her face went pale.

"What is it?" Lin Hao asked.

She didn't answer. She was reading. Her lips moved silently.

The enforcers shifted nervously. One of them reached for his weapon again.

"There's been a development," the woman said finally. Her voice was hollow. "The Butcher's soul signature has been detected. Active. In the mortal realm."

The kitchen felt suddenly very cold.

"He's been alive this whole time," Lin Hao said. It wasn't a question.

"He's been building something." The woman's composure cracked. Just a hairline fracture. But it was there. "A kitchen. Underground. Bigger than yours. He's been stockpiling creatures for years. The fox you bought? That was his bait. He wanted to know if you were still operating."

The cold in Lin Hao's chest spread to his fingers. "Bait for what?"

The woman looked up from her tablet. Her eyes were dark. Empty.

"He's challenging you. The Butcher's issued a culinary duel. Three courses. Winner takes the loser's kitchen, their recipes, and their soul."

The enforcers had gone completely still. The kitchen's lights flickered.

"When?" Lin Hao asked.

"Tomorrow night." The woman's voice dropped to barely a whisper. "And he's already chosen the theme."

"What theme?"

She looked at him like she was delivering a death sentence.

"Phoenix."

The word hung in the air. Lin Hao's mind raced through everything he knew about phoenixes. They were creatures of pure yang energy. Cooking one required techniques no mortal had mastered. The heat alone would vaporize a normal kitchen. The qi release would level a city block.

And the Butcher had been preparing for seven years.

"He's going to serve a phoenix," Lin Hao said slowly. "That's impossible. There hasn't been a confirmed phoenix sighting in three centuries."

"There hasn't been a confirmed sighting." The woman's voice was barely audible. "But there have been rumors. Eggs, hidden in the mountains of Kunlun. Protected by dragons. The Butcher's been digging."

Lin Hao's hands found the edge of his counter. He held on. The wood was warm from a thousand nights of cooking. His grandmother had carved her name into the underside, right where he was gripping now.

He thought about the nine-tailed fox in his cooler. The fenghuang eggs he'd poached last month. The qilin he'd reduced to demi-glace and served to a hungry demon lord who'd wept at the first bite.

He thought about the three-century waiting list. The customers who'd sold their immortal souls for a taste of his cooking.

He thought about losing all of it.

"Tell him I accept."

The woman's eyes widened. "Chef Lin—"

"Tell him." Lin Hao's voice was steady. "Three courses. Tomorrow night. His kitchen or mine?"

"Mine." The word came out like a confession. "He wants it on neutral ground. There's a place. The old slaughterhouse in the industrial district. He's converted it."

Of course he had. The Butcher would want to control every variable. The temperature. The humidity. The ingredients.

The audience.

"How many spectators?"

The woman checked her tablet. "Three hundred confirmed. Celestial nobility. Demon lords. A few gods."

Lin Hao laughed. It came out sharp and bitter. "He's going to humiliate me in front of everyone who matters."

"He's going to destroy you."

"Same thing." Lin Hao turned away. "I need to prep. Mei—clear the schedule. Tell everyone with reservations they'll have to wait."

"You can't win this." The woman's voice was urgent now. "No one has cooked a phoenix in living memory. The techniques are lost. The ingredients are—"

"I know." Lin Hao pulled his knife roll from its drawer. The leather was old and soft. His grandmother had given it to him when he was twelve. "But I'm not cooking a phoenix."

"What?"

He looked at her. In the dim light of his kitchen, surrounded by the ghosts of a thousand meals, he smiled.

"I'm going to cook him."

The woman stared. The enforcers stared. The kitchen hummed.

Lin Hao laid out his knives. One by one. The steel caught the light and threw it back like captured stars.

"The Butcher thinks he's hunting me," he said quietly. "He's been hunting me for seven years. Butchers don't understand chefs. A butcher takes things apart. A chef creates something new." He picked up his favorite knife. The handle was worn smooth by his grip. "Tomorrow night, I'm going to serve him a dish that will change his understanding of what's possible."

"And then?"

Lin Hao set the knife down.

"And then I'm going to cut his goddamn head off."

The phone rang. The red one.

Nobody moved to answer it.

It kept ringing.
